retrieved_docs.title: Erythrocyte Inclusions -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify patients who may benefit from a peripheral blood smear to evaluate possible erythrocyte inclusions based on their clinical history. Identify the limitations of automated analyzers in the evaluation of erythrocyte inclusions. Differentiate among the most commonly encountered erythrocyte inclusions by morphological characteristics and staining patterns. Correlate specific erythrocyte inclusions with potential clinical diagnoses. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Reticulocytes -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Reticulocyte production index (RPI) = [%reticulocyte count x Patient Hct]/45(normal Hct) This is a corrected reticulocyte count used in anemias. In anemias, a falsely elevated reticulocyte count can occur. A decrease in RBCs automatically increases the reticulocyte count even if erythropoiesis has not taken place because the count represents a percentage in comparison to RBCs. It is possible the appropriate increase of erythropoiesis has not taken place in response to the anemia. This value, therefore, corrects for this issue. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Erythrocyte Inclusions -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders that affect erythrocytes can lead to decreased hematopoiesis, erythrocyte shape or size abnormalities, or qualitative and quantitative hemoglobin abnormalities. Erythrocyte inclusions are seen in many hematological disorders, both benign and malignant. Some of the most commonly encountered hematological disorders are anemias, which often have vague and frequently overlapping symptoms. Erythrocyte inclusions may be pathological evidence of specific diseases or disorders when interpreted within the context of presenting clinical symptoms and signs. Characterizing and identifying RBC inclusions can provide or support the diagnosis of various clinical conditions. This activity reviews the morphological characteristics, staining patterns, and clinical correlates of the most commonly encountered erythrocyte inclusions.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Interfering Factors -- Observer Variability
retrieved_docs.content: Microscopic interpretation of PBS and bone marrow morphology is inherently subjective, and results can vary between observers. This variability may lead to discrepancies in the identification and quantification of blasts, potentially impacting diagnosis and risk stratification.

However, the reverse may not always be accurate, emphasizing the importance of thorough clinicopathologic correlation in diagnosis. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Refractory Anemia With Ring Sideroblasts(Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The persistence of this condition is an important communication event in a hematopathologist's diagnostic days.  Patients who develop this condition may be at risk for subsequent progression to AML.  Therefore, communication between the pathologist and hematologist/primary care physician is essential. Once a case of RARs is a consideration as a differential diagnosis, communication that a careful evaluation of the peripheral smear with iron staining performed is in order, as well as ancillary mutational analysis for SF3B. Treatment will often be determined by the clinician shortly after the review of the peripheral blood smear. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Erythrocyte Inclusions -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The routine use of highly accurate and reliable automated hematology analyzers in hematology and clinical pathology laboratories has reduced the use of peripheral blood smears as a diagnostic tool. RBC inclusions can be identified on a peripheral smear, providing concise and valuable clues to the diagnosis or indicating further investigative avenues to arrive at a final diagnosis. The structure, compositional characterization, and pathophysiological link of RBC inclusions may provide valuable clues to underlying disease or disorder. [2] Many erythrocyte inclusions, if present in significant amounts, can interfere with the data obtained from an automated hematology analyzer data. [3] In particular, Heinz bodies and Hemoglobin H can cause erroneous results in automated reticulocyte counts. [4] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Issues of Concern -- Conclusion
retrieved_docs.content: The components of the CBC can help identify a wide range of pathologies and remain one of the most commonly ordered and useful tests in clinical medicine. While automated analyzers have allowed for fast and accurate results, spurious results may arise in several situations (see Table. 3). Differentiating between true changes and spurious abnormalities can impact clinical decision-making and patient care. Results must be interpreted in the clinical context and according to local laboratory guidelines, as reference intervals are specific to each lab. An examination of a peripheral blood smear is essential in assessing any abnormal CBC result and is recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Bone Marrow -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Dysplasia
retrieved_docs.content: Erythroid dysplasia is characterized by morphological abnormalities, including nuclear budding and lobation, internuclear bridges, karyorrhexis, binuclear and multinuclearity, cytoplasmic vacuolation, and megaloblastic changes, and the presence of ringed sideroblasts (see Image. Nuclear Budding). [12] Nonneoplastic conditions like megaloblastic anemia, congenital dyserythropoietic anemia, copper deficiency, and zinc excess may also exhibit features of erythroid dysplasia. Ringed sideroblasts may be detected by iron-staining aspirate smears with particles. A semiquantitative score of 1 to 4 is used to evaluate the abundance of ringed sideroblasts.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Approach to Interpretation of CBC
retrieved_docs.content: To understand complete blood count, it is important to understand the process of hemopoiesis. Hemopoiesis involves forming mature blood cells from immature cells and consists of a series of distinctively recognizable cells. The production of RBCs, WBCs, platelets, and lymphocytes are named erythropoiesis, granulopoiesis, thrombopoiesis, and lymphopoiesis, respectively. There is a balance between the production and destruction of cells, and each cell has a normal range of count in normal human blood.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Anemias
retrieved_docs.content: A decrease in hemoglobin level or total circulating red cell mass for a specified age and sex of a person is called anemia. Clinical features include fatigue, weakness, shortness of breath, palpitations, dizziness, headache, and decreased exercise tolerance. Diagnostic tests for anemias: The following initial investigations are performed if anemia is suspected: CBC with differential Retic count Peripheral blood smear Morphologically, anemias are classified into three major types

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Bone Marrow -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Discordance Between Aspirate and Bone Marrow Trephine Biopsy
retrieved_docs.content: Discrepancies between the results of bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy samples occur frequently. Bone marrow aspirate criteria for the morphological assessment of blasts and erythroid or granulocytic dysplasia are more clearly defined than biopsy criteria. Focal lesions such as lymphoid and plasma cell aggregation, metastatic carcinomas, and dysplastic megakaryocyte growth can be underrepresented in aspirates and better appreciated on trephine biopsies. Dry marrow taps attributed to pathologies like myelofibrosis, aplastic anemia, and metastatic carcinoma infiltration are also better visualized on trephine biopsy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1803
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings” includes specific abnormal results such as a positive culture (المزرعة إيجابية) and an abnormal chromosome analysis (تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي). These particular findings are considered sub‑categories of the broader group of nonspecific abnormal laboratory results, indicating that when such tests return these outcomes, they represent distinct types of abnormal cytological, histological, immunological, or DNA evidence that require further clinical interpretation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1152
retrieved_docs.content: Acute anemia resulting from blood loss and anemia resulting from blood loss are both classified as members of the “Bleeding terms (excluding laboratory terms) (SMQ)” category, indicating that they belong to the same standardized group of bleeding‑related concepts. Hemorrhagic infiltration is clinically associated with anemia resulting from blood loss, reflecting that the presence of blood‑filled tissue infiltration often co‑occurs with or contributes to the development of anemia due to loss of blood. This interrelationship highlights how specific bleeding manifestations and their resulting anemic states are organized within a common terminology framework and are linked in clinical presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 218
retrieved_docs.content: the diagnostic realm, the term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation for both abnormal amniocentesis (البزل السّلوي غير الطبيعي) and Fanconi anemia (فقر دم فانكوني), indicating that the presence of atypical findings in amniotic fluid analysis or hematologic evaluation is flagged as abnormal. Overall, these relationships illustrate a structured taxonomy in obstetrics: broad pregnancy complications encompass specific anesthesia‑related maternal morbidities, fluid and membrane disorders, and labor‑related conditions; each of these entities is further classified into more precise diagnostic or outcome categories, facilitating comprehensive patient assessment and targeted management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 585
retrieved_docs.content: Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings encompass a broad spectrum of atypical laboratory results, and within this category a positive culture (المزرعة إيجابية) is identified as a specific child finding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2441
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation for both the Arabic phrase “البزل السّلوي غير الطبيعي” and the condition “فقر دم فانكوني.” In clinical practice, describing a spinal tap as “البزل السّلوي غير الطبيعي” indicates an abnormal cerebrospinal fluid result, while labeling “فقر دم فانكوني” as abnormal reflects the presence of Fanconi anemia, a hereditary bone‑marrow failure syndrome. Recognizing that “Abnormal” serves as a common descriptor for these distinct entities highlights its role as a generic marker of pathological or atypical findings in patient evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2450
retrieved_docs.content: Deficiency and other anemia is related to فقر الدم الحاد الناتج عن فقدان الدم, which denotes severe anemia resulting from blood loss. فقر الدم الحاد الناتج عن فقدان الدم is included as a member of مصطلحات النزف (باستثناء المصطلحات المختبرية) (SMQ), the standardized set of bleeding terms that excludes laboratory‑specific terminology. This relationship places severe blood‑loss anemia within the broader category of deficiency‑type anemias and aligns it with the clinical coding framework for bleeding disorders, facilitating accurate identification and management of patients experiencing significant hemorrhagic anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2004
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation of فقر دم فانكوني (Fanconi anemia), indicating that the clinical and laboratory findings associated with this genetic disorder are considered abnormal. Fanconi anemia is a hereditary condition characterized by bone‑marrow failure, congenital anomalies, and a heightened risk of malignancies, and the descriptor “Abnormal” reflects the pathological nature of its presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 694
retrieved_docs.content: Microscopic examination of specimen from spleen and of bone marrow, culture; Microscopic examination of specimen from spleen and of bone marrow, culture and sensitivity; and Microscopic examination of specimen from spleen and of bone marrow, toxicology are laboratory procedures that are performed on bone marrow (kostní dřeň). Bone marrow (kostní dřeň) is also the anatomical site where aplastic anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) is identified, indicating that these examinations can be used to evaluate marrow pathology and detect or monitor aplastic anemia. Integrating these tests provides a comprehensive assessment of marrow health and related hematologic disorders.

This integrated view supports comprehensive diagnostic reasoning and coordinated patient management across hematologic, ophthalmologic, and autoimmune domains.

Recognizing these interconnections, the associated syndromes, and the relevant laboratory parameters (e.g., total hemoglobin) is essential for accurate diagnosis, interpretation of test results, and targeted management of infants during the neonatal and early infancy periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings represent a broad category of atypical laboratory results, and within this category the specific finding تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي (abnormal chromosome analysis) is included as a child or sub‑type, indicating that abnormal chromosome analysis is one of the nonspecific abnormal test results encompassed by this broader classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: The community classifies anemia associated with chronic kidney disease under the broader category of other deficiency anemias, which also comprises folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات, also called فقر الدم الوبيل), iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات), non‑regenerative (aplastic) anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية), and cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد). Thalassemia (الثلاسيميا) is also placed within this group despite being a hemoglobinopathy, and it shares the ocular manifestation of sickle‑cell‑related retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية).
